markdown:
## Meaning of market power


<definition term="Market power">The degree to which a **firm** in a **market** is able to **control** **its** **output** **price**.</definition>

1. **Market power **is the degree to which firms are able to **control** **the** **price** at which they sell their goods and services on the market.
2. Different **market** **structures** have different degrees of **market** **power.**
3. **Market structures c**an be categorised by looking at each of the following characteristics:
  1. **Number of firms in the market.**
  2. **Product differentiation**: how similar (or different) are the goods or services offered.
  3. **Barriers of entry**: how hard is it to enter the market as a new firm.
  4. **Degree of market power**.
4. Looking at these characteristics, there are four main types of **market structures**:
  1. **Perfect competition → no market power.**
  2. **Monopolistic competition → some market power.**
  3. **Oligopoly → a lot of market power.**
  4. **Monopoly → complete market power.**

<callout type="note">In this section, we will discuss the characteristics of **perfect competition**.</callout>

## Perfect competition: no market power, firm as price taker


<definition term="Perfect competition">A **market structure** with a **large** **number** **of** **small** **firms** that have **no control over output prices**. All firms sell an **undifferentiated** **product** and there are **no barriers to entry**.</definition>

**Firms **in a **perfect competition** **market**:

1. Are **small-sized** and there are **many firms in the market**.
2. Sell an **undifferentiated product**.
3. Can easily enter the market **without barriers to entry**.
4. **Sell their products at the price demanded by the market**.

As a **market structure**,** perfect competition markets **have the following characteristics:

1. **Maximise profits **in the **long-run**.
2. **Achieve allocative efficiency**.

### Profit maximisation in the short run


1. In the previous subtopic we saw that **profit maximisation **occurs when **MB = MC**.
2. If we combine all the **marginal and average costs and revenues, as well as supply and demand** into one single diagram (_Figure 1)_, we can see the different types of profits (normal, abnormal, and losses) firms can make when producing at a **profit maximisation level of output **($Q_{max}$).
3. In the **short-run**, firms in **perfect competition** can be making **any kinds of profit **(abnormal, normal, losses).

![Figure 1: short-run profit maximisation outcomes for firms in perfect competition markets](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/594940e37b4cf502cf70ab976bdd9a01236a0382-2517x4934.png)

<callout type="tip">###  Interpreting _Figure 1_


_Figure 1_ illustrates three firms in perfect competition markets in the short-run:

  1. One making **normal profits**.
  2. One making **abnormal profits**.
  3. One making **losses**.

#### The axis


1. The **x axis** represents:
  1. Quantity demanded.
  2. Quantity produced (sold).
2. This is because all those measures are expressed in terms of **quantity**.
3. The **y axis** represents:
  1. Price.
  2. Revenue.
  3. Cost.
4. This is because all those measures are expressed in terms of **currency**.

#### The demand curve


The **demand curve** is **horizontal** because in **perfect competition markets** firms take the **price determined by the market**.

1. Imagine a local market where there are 20 fruit stores that sell oranges of the exact same quality (undifferentiated).
2. The supply and demand forces of the market agree on the equilibrium price, and the firms in the **perfect competition** market must sell at this price (_Figure 2)_.
3. If a firm sells oranges at a higher price than the rest, no one will buy them (assuming rational consumer behaviour).
4. If a firms lowers its output prices, they lose profits.

![Figure 2: firms in perfect competition markets must price their output at the equilibrium price](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/48324e878067562f7aff52a0738aa15396da1b29-2365x1002.png)

Therefore, the **demand **a firm faces in a **perfect competition market** is **perfectly elastic**, and equals its **output price**, **marginal revenue**, **and average revenue **(to understand why **$D=P=MR=AR$**, see _Table 4** **_in _2.11.4_).

#### Abnormal profit


1. Firms always produce at $Q_{max}$, where **profit maximisation** occurs. This point is when $MR=MC$, so when the **MR and MC curves intersect**.
2. However, at this $Q_{max}$, **$AR > AC_1$**, and so the firm is making **abnormal profit**.
3. The **average profit per unit **equals the difference, at $Q_{max}$ between the **average revenue **(equal to $P_{e}$)** **and** **the **average cost **($AC_1$).
4. The **total profit** equal the **average profit **($P_{e}-AC_1$) times the **units** **produced** ($Q_{max}$):

$$(P_{e}-AC_1) \times Q_{max}$$

This is visualised by the red box.

#### Normal profit


1. Firms always produce at $Q_{max}$, where **profit maximisation** occurs. This point is when $MR=MC$, so when the **MR and MC curves intersect**.
2. At this $Q_{max}$, $**AR = AC_2$**, and so the firm is making **normal profit**.
3. The **average profit per unit **equals the difference between the **average revenue **(equal to $P_{e}$)** **and** **the **average cost **($AC_2$). 
4. Since $P_{e}=AC_2$ in this case, the firm is making **normal (zero) profit**.

#### Losses


1. Firms always produce at $Q_{max}$, where **profit maximisation** occurs. This point is when $MR=MC$, so when the **MR and MC curves intersect**.
2. However, at this $Q_{max}$, **$AR < AC_3$**, and so the firm is making **losses**.
3. The **average profit per unit **equals the difference between the **average revenue **(equal to $P_{e}$)** **and** **the **average cost **(AC_3).
4. The **total profit** equal the **average profit **($P_{e}-AC_3$) times the **units** **produced** ($Q_{max}$):

$$(P_{e}-AC_3) \times Q_{max}$$

However, since **$AC>P_{max}$**, the** total profit** is **negative**, the firm is making **losses**.** **These **total** **losses** are visualised by the red box.</callout>

### Profit maximisation in the long run


<callout type="note">In the **long run**, all firms in a **perfect competition market** make **normal profits**.</callout>

1. In the **long run**, **all factors are variable** → firms can **enter, exit, or change size**, meaning market conditions **adjust** **over** **time**.
2. If the firms in **perfect competition market** are making **abnormal profits**, they will **attract new firms** to the market → increased **market supply lowers prices**, reducing **AR** until only **normal profit (AR = AC)** remains.
3. Contrarily, if firms face **losses for a long time, **some will** exit the market** → **supply decreases**, raising prices and **AR** until remaining firms earn **normal profit**.

### Allocative efficiency of perfect competition


<definition term="Allocative Efficiency">Allocative efficiency refers to the situation where the market produces the socially desirable quantity of output.</definition>

1. As we discussed in previous sections, **allocative efficiency** is reached, when:
  1. **Marginal Benefit (MB) = Marginal Cost (MC)**.
  2. This occurs when **P = MC**.
2. In the **long-run**, in **perfect competition markets**, all firms produce at a point where **P = MC**.
3. Therefore, **perfect competition** leads to **allocative efficiency**.

<callout type="note">Note however that this statement is true only when there are **no externalities in the market**.</callout>

### Evaluating perfect competition


#### Advantages of perfect competition


1. **Allocative efficiency in the long run:**
  1. In perfect competition, firms produce at the point where **Price (P) = Marginal Cost (MC)**, ensuring **allocative efficiency**.
  2. This means resources are distributed in a way that maximises **consumer and societal welfare**: firms produce exactly what consumers demand at the lowest possible price.
2. **Low prices for consumers:**
  1. Since **abnormal profits are eliminated in the long run**, firms cannot charge excessive prices.
  2. The **absence of market power** ensures that firms **price goods at the lowest sustainable cost**, benefiting consumers.
3. **Competitive pressure eliminates inefficiency:**
  1. Inefficient firms (those with **higher costs or lower quality production) **are forced out of the market.
  2. Only firms that **minimise costs and operate efficiently** can survive in the long run, leading to **higher productivity and innovation**.
4. **Market dynamism: responding to consumer demand**
  1. In the long run, firms **adjust their production** based on **changes in consumer preferences**.
  2. If demand for a good increases, **new firms enter**, increasing supply and stabilising prices.
  3. If demand falls, **firms exit the market**, reducing supply and preventing excess production.
  4. This automatic adjustment ensures that **resources flow to industries where they are most needed**.

<callout type="note">Overall, **perfect competition **achieves **allocative efficiency**, **lowers consumer prices**, and **improves the productivity** of the economy by **cutting off inefficient producers**.</callout>

#### Limitations of perfect competition


1. **Unrealistic assumptions:**
  1. Perfect competition assumes **infinite buyers and sellers**, **perfect information**, and **no barriers to entry or exit**, which **rarely exist** in actual markets.
  2. Real-world firms often have **brand loyalty, government regulations, and imperfect knowledge**, all of which distort market outcomes.
2. **No economies of scale lead to higher average costs:**
  1. Since firms are **small and price takers**, they **cannot expand production significantly** to benefit from **economies of scale** (cost savings from large-scale production).
  2. Larger firms in **monopolies or oligopolies** can often produce at **lower average costs**, making goods cheaper in some cases compared to a perfectly competitive market.
3. **Lack of product variety:**
  1. Perfect competition assumes **homogeneous (identical) products**, meaning there is **no product differentiation**.
  2. In reality, consumers value **brand identity, quality differences, and unique features**, which do not exist in perfect competition.
  3. Monopolistic competition, where firms differentiate their products, often **better reflects real consumer preferences**.
4. **Limited innovation and product development:**
  1. Since firms only earn **normal profit (AR = AC) in the long run**, there is **little incentive for research, innovation, or product development**.
  2. Without **excess profits**, firms cannot fund **new technology or improvements**, limiting long-term progress.
  3. In contrast, markets with **monopolies or oligopolies** may invest more in **R&D** because of their **higher profits**.

<callout type="note">While **perfect competition** is **theoretically efficient**, its **real-world limitations** make it **rarely applicable**. While it provides **valuable insights into market behaviour**, most industries operate under **imperfect competition**, where **innovation, differentiation, and economies of scale** play a crucial role.</callout>

<callout type="case_study">### Perfect competition: Wheat farming in the US


**When**: Ongoing (20th century to  present).

**Where**: United States.

**What**: The U.S. wheat industry closely resembles **perfect competition**, with over **250,000 wheat farms** producing a **homogeneous product** and acting as **price takers** in a competitive global market. In 2022, the U.S. produced **44.9 million metric tons of wheat**, making it the **fourth-largest wheat producer globally**.

#### Advantages of perfect competition


1. **Allocative efficiency**: farmers sell at the **market price (P = MC)**, ensuring resources are efficiently allocated. The U.S. wheat market operates under **global price fluctuations**, with wheat prices averaging **$8.83 per bushel in 2022**.
2. **Low prices for consumers**: high competition keeps wheat prices **low and stable**, benefiting consumers. In 2022, wheat exports accounted for **50% of total U.S. wheat production**, with major buyers including **Mexico, Japan, and the Philippines**.
3. **Competitive pressure**: inefficient farmers exit the market, leading to **higher productivity**. **Advancements in farming technology** have allowed yields to increase, with the average U.S. wheat yield reaching **48.6 bushels per acre in 2022**.
4. **Market responsiveness**: if wheat demand rises, **more farmers plant wheat,** if demand falls, **less profitable farms close**. For example, U.S. wheat exports **declined by 10% in 2021** due to reduced global demand, leading some farmers to switch to alternative crops like soybeans.

#### Limitations of perfect competition


* **Unrealistic assumptions**: farmers **lack perfect information**, and **government subsidies ($6 billion in 2022) and trade policies** influence production.
* **No economies of scale**: small farmers **struggle to lower costs**, while large agribusinesses like **Cargill and Archer Daniels Midland** dominate global wheat trade.
* **Lack of product variety**: wheat is **homogeneous**, so farmers **compete only on price**, unlike **monopolistic competition**, where branding matters.
* **Limited innovation**: small farms earn **only normal profits**, limiting investment in **research and development (R&D)**. Despite technological advances, total U.S. wheat acreage has **declined by 40% since the 1980s**, as many farmers shift to more profitable crops.

#### So?:


1. **Advantage**: U.S. wheat farming benefits from **low prices, efficiency, and high productivity** due to competition.
2. **Limitation**: **Economies of scale, government subsidies, and external market forces prevent** it from being a purely "perfectly competitive" industry.
3. **Policy challenges**: Small wheat farmers **depend on government support**, and global trade conditions continue to shape U.S. wheat competitiveness.</callout>

## Imperfect competition: varying degrees of market power, firm as price maker


All **market structures **where **firms **can individually determine their output prices (price makers) are forms of **imperfect competition**. These include:

1. **Monopolistic competition**.
2. **Oligopoly**.
3. **Monopoly**.

Since **imperfect competition firms** are **price makers:**

1. They have a **non-horizontal demand curve** because they must **lower prices to sell more units**.
2. Their **average revenue (AR)** is different to its **marginal revenue (MR) **(see _Table 5 _in _subtopic 2.11.4_).
3. These differences are illustrated in _Figure 3_ below, showcasing firms in **imperfect competition** making **abnormal, normal, and negative profit**.

![Figure 3: profit maximisation outcomes for firms in imperfect competition markets](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/fcd39bc7920ace680e797474917e40f73af64501-2517x4934.png)

### Allocative inefficiency in the long run


1. If all firms in an **imperfect competition market** make **normal profits** (which is not always the case), they produce at a point where $**P \neq MC$ **(_Figure 3_)**.**
2. Therefore,** **even in the **long run**, **imperfect competition** markets lead to **allocative inefficiency. **

<callout type="note">We will explore the different types of **imperfect competition** in the upcoming subtopics, as well as their **advantages** and **disadvantages**.</callout>

****
